items.prompt_md: In the trie above, what marks the difference between a node that is just a shared prefix (like the `an` in `ant`) and a node that is an actual stored word?
options: Whether the node has any children at all
The depth of the node from the root
Whether the node's dict is empty
items.explanation_md: A node can have children (more letters follow) and still be a complete word — `is_word` is what `search` actually checks, independent of whether the path continues further.

items.prompt_md: You must support inserting words and then repeatedly answering "does any stored word start with this prefix?" for many different prefixes. Which approach fits?
options: Store the words in a `set` and scan all of them for each prefix query
Sort the words and binary-search for the prefix each time
Build a prefix tree (trie) keyed by character, one node per letter along the path
Hash each whole word and compare hashes for equality
items.answer: Build a prefix tree (trie) keyed by character, one node per letter along the path
items.explanation_md: Repeated prefix queries against a shared/growing dictionary is the trie fingerprint: each query costs O(L) — the length of the prefix — no matter how many words are stored, because shared prefixes are stored once as a shared path.

items.prompt_md: A trie holds N words with a maximum length L. What is the time complexity of a single `insert`, `search`, or `starts_with` call?
items.explanation_md: Each operation walks at most L characters from the root, one dict lookup/insert per character, so the cost depends only on the query's length, not on how many words N are stored in the trie.
